keyword.primary: passed inburgering but can't speak Dutch
keyword.searcher_intent: Someone who has passed the exam or knows people who did, frustrated that passing didn't equal speaking ability. Looking for explanation and path forward.

audience.pain_summary: Has the certificate but not the ability. Feels like a fraud. May have wasted money on a course that taught passing, not speaking.

angle: Validate the frustration, explain why this is systemic (not personal failure), and show there's still a path to actual speaking ability. The certificate was step one—real Dutch comes next.
thesis_sentence: Passing the inburgering exam doesn't mean you can speak Dutch—and that's not your fault; it's how the system and most courses are designed.

outline.notes: Open with the specific frustration. The certificate is official—but you still freeze when someone speaks to you in Dutch. You can read signs but can't order coffee. The gap between 'passed' and 'fluent' is real. Use Nina's friend story: 'He had just gotten his passport. He couldn't even pronounce inburgering.'

Explain the structural problem. Exam tests recognition (can you understand?) more than production (can you speak?). Even speaking exam can be prepared with memorized responses. Courses optimize for passing because that's what customers ask for. The result: 'People don't even expect that at A2 you can have a conversation.'
outline.evidence_needed: Nina quote on low expectations, market alert on normalized non-fluency

outline.notes: Most self-paced courses complete when you finish exercises. They train recognition: watch video, do quiz, next lesson. Speaking might be tested, but it's not required as proof of completion. 'InburgeringOnline is just a bunch of things to memorize... cheat sheet to the exam.' Result: certificate without capability.
outline.evidence_needed: Nina on InburgeringOnline, competitive landscape on recognition-based completion

outline.notes: Go deeper than the practical. Feeling like a fraud. 'If I get my citizenship but I can't speak at all... I would always feel like an outsider. I don't feel I deserve that passport.' The shame of having the certificate but not the skill. This isn't about the exam—it's about identity and belonging.

outline.section: Building Real Speaking Ability (After the Exam)
outline.notes: Practical path forward. The good news: you have vocabulary locked in passive memory. The task: convert recognition to production. Methods: conversation practice with level-appropriate partners, output before input, using words in context not isolation. Thread Dutchwithjoost as option for those who want structured production practice.

outline.section: What to Do Differently Next Time
outline.notes: For readers who haven't passed yet or need B1. Advice: choose courses based on how they handle completion. Ask: 'What do I need to do to finish a lesson?' If it's 'click through,' expect recognition. If it's 'demonstrate production,' expect to actually learn to speak.

value_props_to_weave.prop: Lessons complete when you can have conversations on the topic
value_props_to_weave.proof: Nina friend who 'couldn't pronounce inburgering' after passing
value_props_to_weave.approved_phrasing: A word counts as learned when you've used it in conversation, not when you've seen it. You pass the exam because you can actually use Dutch.

value_props_to_weave.prop: The exam becomes straightforward because you've actually learned the language
value_props_to_weave.proof: Nina: 'I don't feel I deserve that passport'
value_props_to_weave.approved_phrasing: You learn vocabulary by using it, not memorizing it. The exam becomes a natural result of what you've learned.

value_props_to_weave.prop: Joost speaks at your level and only uses words you've already practiced
value_props_to_weave.proof: Nina: 'It's like speaking to somebody who is good in Dutch but only at my level.'
value_props_to_weave.approved_phrasing: Joost speaks at your level. The conversation changes based on what you say. It's not repeating a script.

prohibited_language: Do NOT say 'the exam only tests recognition' — the exam tests both recognition AND production
Do NOT say competitors are 'cheat sheets' or use dismissive language
Do NOT imply memorization courses are lazy or easy to build
Do NOT say 'AI tutor' without context — describe what Joost does
originality_nuggets: Friend story: 'He had just gotten his passport. He couldn't even pronounce inburgering.'
'People don't even expect that at A2 you can have a conversation'—normalized failure
'I don't feel I deserve that passport'—emotional depth of the 'pass but can't speak' problem

seo.title_tag: Passed Inburgering But Can't Speak Dutch? Here's Why
seo.meta_description: Passed the inburgering exam but still can't hold a conversation? You're not alone. Here's why the exam doesn't equal speaking ability—and what to do next.

aeo_directives.featured_snippet_target: Why can't I speak Dutch after passing inburgering?
aeo_directives.definition_paragraph: Many people pass the inburgering exam but cannot speak Dutch conversationally because the exam and most preparation courses focus on recognition (understanding Dutch) rather than production (speaking Dutch). Courses optimize for passing because that's what customers request, but passing the A2 exam does not require conversational fluency—it requires meeting specific test criteria.
faq_questions: Is it normal to pass inburgering but not speak Dutch?
Why doesn't passing inburgering mean I can speak?
How do I learn to speak Dutch after passing inburgering?
Did my inburgering course fail me?
Can you get Dutch citizenship without speaking Dutch?
